content: Beta-thalassemia is an inherited blood disorder characterized by reduced or absent synthesis of the beta chains of hemoglobin, resulting in variable outcomes ranging from severe anemia to clinically asymptomatic individuals. It is caused by mutations in the HBB gene on chromosome 11.

A 'Pathogenic' classification means the variant is recognized as disease-causing. In the context of the HBB gene, pathogenic variants include nonsense, frameshift, and canonical splice site mutations (often leading to beta0-thalassemia with no beta-globin production), as well as certain missense mutations. Homozygous or compound heterozygous inheritance of pathogenic variants typically results in Beta-Thalassemia Major or Intermedia.

A 'Benign' classification means the variant does not cause disease. These are often common polymorphisms found at high frequencies in the general population (e.g., in gnomAD) and typically do not alter protein function significantly (e.g., synonymous variants or deep intronic variants without splice effects).

A 'Variant of Uncertain Significance' (VUS) means there is currently insufficient or conflicting evidence to classify the variant as either pathogenic or benign. Clinical correlation, functional studies, or family segregation analysis may be required to determine its true effect.

Beta-Thalassemia Major (Cooley's Anemia) is the most severe form. It occurs when a patient inherits two severe (usually beta0) mutations. Patients present with severe life-threatening anemia within the first two years of life, requiring lifelong regular blood transfusions and iron chelation therapy. Complications include iron overload, splenomegaly, and bone deformities.

Beta-Thalassemia Intermedia presents with a wide spectrum of severity, falling between Major and Minor. Patients typically inherit two mild (beta+) mutations or one severe and one mild mutation. They have moderate anemia and do not require regular lifelong transfusions, though they may need them occasionally (e.g., during infection or pregnancy). Iron overload can still occur due to increased intestinal iron absorption.

Beta-Thalassemia Minor (Carrier trait) occurs when a patient inherits one defective HBB allele and one normal allele (heterozygous state). Individuals are typically asymptomatic but may have mild microcytic anemia. It is generally discovered incidentally on routine blood work. Genetic counseling is advised for family planning.

Splice site mutations in the HBB gene often disrupt normal mRNA processing. Canonical splice site mutations (at the conserved GT donor or AG acceptor sites) typically abolish normal splicing, leading to a beta0 phenotype (no functional beta-globin). Other splice region mutations may lead to reduced splicing efficiency, resulting in a beta+ phenotype.

Nonsense and frameshift mutations in the HBB gene lead to premature termination codons. These usually trigger nonsense-mediated mRNA decay, resulting in no beta-globin production (beta0 phenotype). These are among the most severe classes of mutations.

ACMG criteria for pathogenicity include: population frequency (very low or absent supports pathogenicity), computational predictions (e.g., CADD, SpliceAI, phyloP indicating deleterious effects), functional assay data, and clinical observations (e.g., segregation with disease). A variant is considered pathogenic if it meets specific combinations of very strong, strong, moderate, and supporting evidence.
